# Guillermo Rauch’s Post
5mo
Vercel is 𝘰𝘧𝘧𝘪𝘤𝘪𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘺 10 years old, according to the State of Delaware, the first US 🇺🇸 state 🫡. Some reflections from the journey: 1️⃣ You can just incorporate things "In America, they let you start companies easily". It's magical how fast you can get your ', 𝙸𝚗𝚌', your bank account, your LOIs, your payroll set up… Docusigns fly, investors are eager to take risk, there's an overwhelming sense of optimism around technology. Business owners are the heroes of this society. We must preserve this company-building culture at all costs. Thankful to San Francisco for being the epitome of it. 2️⃣ 10 years is the baseline It's wild to me that it feels like Vercel is just getting started. I remember prior to starting the company looking at the statistics on the median time from inception to IPO for tech companies. It was 8-10 years then and now looking like 10-14. There are many tough moments while building a company. But what's a bad day or quarter going to do if you know this is a 15-year journey? 3️⃣ It's always day zero When I registered the domain name and shipped the first company landing page, there was no Next.js. No Git integration. No CDN. No Turborepo. No AI SDK. No v0. At one point, Jamstack and static sites were all the rage. Now, agents and AI. The only way to survive in this game is to have tremendous adaptability and appetite for shipping. Our first name was "Zeit", because the only way to win is to capture the Zeitgeist. As Malte Ubl likes to say, iteration velocity and shipping is the solution to all known s̶o̶f̶t̶w̶a̶r̶e̶ ̶e̶n̶g̶i̶n̶e̶e̶r̶i̶n̶g̶ problems in the world.
I really like this viewpoint. It's always day 0 .no company is too big to change and innovate if there is a culture to do that . Failure is part of life !

# How to build a network without "networking"
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Vercel•57K followers
6mo
A lot of younger people who’re new to the industry ask me how to do “networking”. How do you go from knowing no one in San Francisco to having a network of people that you can do business with, learn from, hire, etc. The trick is I never set out to “network”. Maybe due to Tylenol exposure in womb, or just pure intuition, but I could never fathom that I’d go to an event or reach out to someone to “network”. “Hi this is G and I’m here to network”. Instead what I focused on was creating value for others and focus on positive value exchanges. Creating open source projects is the “hyper scale” version of this. You’re networking with the world, but you’re doing so by leading with value and with content. Writing essays, teaching people things, giving lightning talks, vibe coding an experiment, all more valuable. What you’re going to notice is that networks build around value, not the other way around. Even today, whenever I reach out to someone, I try to lead with some insight, datapoint, maybe a bug report or suggestion for their product… something tangible. If they have a product or platform with APIs, I start by networking *our systems*. I’ve made amazing, close connections to people who I frequently chat with and I’ve never met IRL. I also make it a point to talk to everybody. Anons, interns, juniors, seniors, CEOs, board members, trolls. tl;DR: Focus on creating value, think long term, don’t be transactional.

Am I working with people who help me grow as a thinker? Does this project help me show that I get things done? Will this opportunity build networks in the fields I care about? At EAG London 2025, I asked a dozen professionals what made the biggest difference in landing their roles. Most worked in small EA-aligned nonprofits (5–25 people) as operations, project, or program managers. I wanted to know what skills or experience I should focus on if I’d like to follow a similar path. Here are the three answers that came up most often: 1. Networks — An obvious one, but worth highlighting. Hiring someone you’ve already seen in action feels far less risky. Tip: It’s easier to build networks if you have something concrete to ask for (e.g. feedback on a project idea). 2. Getting shit done — Straightforward, but crucial. Results speak for themselves. 3. Clear, critical thinking — This one was more novel to me. Small organisations need people who see the bigger picture and think independently about how to reach goals effectively. A few even challenged assumptions during the hiring process, not to be difficult, but to make sure the role and their skills were genuinely a good fit. With this in mind, since stepping down from EA Finland to start university last month, I’ve tried to find people and ideas to build something together from 0 to 1. 👉 Is there any 4th point you would add to the list?

# How Vercel boosts AI and human performance with context
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
6mo Edited
It's now well understood that the better the context, the better the performance of AI models. But it's the same lesson as scaling human organizations. Maximize and enrich context. Engineers and designers are not code & pixels pushers. They solve business problems. At Vercel we've found that maximizing transparency and a 'build in public' ethos increases autonomy (agency 😁) and makes work more fulfilling and impactful. This is incidentally how we end up with stories of interns engineering infrastructure improvements that save millions upon millions of dollars for our customers: vercel.fyi/stampede

Alexander Obenauer has a new series on the need for composability in operating systems. His latest is https://lnkd.in/e4aTFTK4 He's a good thinker about this stuff. And I resonate with the mission. Unlike Obenauer I suspect, I am much more wedded to text than graphics and graphic design. I love text interfaces, and one reason for that is that I just think it's much easier to do all the other good things like abstraction and composition with text than it is in a graphical environment. The Unix pipeline has just worked for decades. There are millions of ad hoc "applications" created on the fly, every day, by people piping together some Unix tools. It's so good you don't notice it. Composing GUI elements is much harder. You can kind of embed one thing in another by having one app allocate a canvas to act as a window to another. These days, it's not uncommon to use iframes in web pages to compose from multiple sources. But full integration and communication between them is hard. Admittedly partly because of necessary security concerns. But also UI elements can look different. Keyboard short-cuts can clash. And size and shape constraints from both the original app and the host app are not necessarily compatible. Web design is, when you come to think of it, a surprisingly large amount of work, just trying to fit components that come from one app, together visually. Let alone two apps. And if you hoped to nest three apps, one inside another? And if your ideal is that end users can compose multiple apps together on the fly? That's why I personally believe in text. And it's one of the reasons I was excited when the original ChatGPT appeared. Not only because of the AI. But because a chat window seems to me to be the best way to get something closer to the Unix terminal experience out to a large number of mainstream users. If I were to sit down to try to create a new UI shell for a new operating system, it would start with a chatbot as the main front-end. Internally it would deal with something like the "Intents" you have on Android. Where multiple "apps" can register to handle the same data type or task. The chatbot would route natural language requests to these apps and replies in text. Sometimes the app might need to show ad hoc GUI components (eg a drawing canvas, a colour picker) as and when needed. Behind the scenes, the bot would manage installing and routing data between the apps; storing / publishing final results etc.
The moral imperative for composable systems alexanderobenauer.com

🚀 Exploring SystemVerilog Enums! 🚀 Today, I dived into SystemVerilog enumerations (enums) and had some fun experimenting with their built-in methods. Enums allow us to define a set of named values instead of relying on raw numbers — which makes designs more readable and maintainable. Here’s what I explored: .first() & .last() → Access the first and last enum elements .num() → Count the number of elements in the enum .name() → Get the name of an enum element .next() & .prev() → Traverse forward and backward through enum elements *Code:* module tb; typedef enum{red, blue, green, yellow, white, black} my_enum; my_enum vs; my_enum temp; initial begin $display("--------------------------------------------------------"); $display("value of first element of enum = %0d \n", vs.first()); $display("value of last element of enum = %0d \n", vs.last()); $display("number of element in enum = %0d \n", vs.num()); $display("name of enum = %0s \n", vs.name()); vs = green; $display("next index value of green = %0d \n", vs.next()); $display("previous index value of green = %0d \n", vs.prev()); $display("elements of enum are :"); temp = vs.first(); do begin $write("%s, ", temp.name()); temp = temp.next(); end while(temp != vs.first()); $display("\n"); temp = white; temp = temp.next(); $display("next element of white is = %0s \n", temp.name()); vs = vs.prev(); $display("previous element of green is = %0s \n", vs.name()); $display("--------------------------------------------------------"); end endmodule I even looped through all enum elements using a do-while loop, which is super handy for iterating without manually keeping track of indexes. ✅ Why this matters: Enums improve code readability, reduce bugs from magic numbers, and allow clean iteration over states or modes in your digital designs. Feeling excited to apply these in finite state machines (FSMs) and complex RTL designs!#SystemVerilog#VLSI#DigitalDesign#FPGA#RTLDesign#HardwareVerification#Engineering

The Internet is Broken and the Way We Build It is to Blame We are in an age where we have AI powerful enough to write code, clouds that scale infinitely and hardware that can handle billions of operations per second. Yet somehow, the average website is broken, leaks user data, burns through CPU and breaks on half the browsers. The problem isn't the internet or AI. It's the culture around how we build. We've layered convenience over craftsmanship. We obsess over frameworks, not fundamentals. We chase deadlines over durability. We treat software like disposable fashion, not infrastructure. The internet wasn't meant to be a bloated maze of APIs, JS bundles and UI libraries stitched together with duct tape. It was meant to be fast, open and resilient and a space where engineering meant elegance, not excess. Good engineering is invisible. Bad engineering is everywhere, in every spinning loader, every memory leak, every "please refresh the page" moment that reminds us just how fragile the modern web has become. Maybe it's not the engineers. Maybe it's the pressure to ship, not shape. And somewhere in that rush, the internet lost a bit of its soul.

Software ages like milk, not wine ✓ Visualizing traces of time is the best way to optimize it ✓ Ship frequently and strive to build in public ✓ Errors must have globally unique codes & hyperlinks ✓ Red is not enough to signal "error" (8% of men have red-green color blindness)

If performance is critical to a system, performance needs to be an integral part of the design. It doesn't mean that all code should be optimized from the beginning, but it should be clear that the initial design can meet the performance criteria. Writing a huge system and then starting to measure/optimize could mean a full rewrite. In high-throughput or low-latency systems, you typically start with a basic core that meets the performance criteria. With the core in place, new functionality can be added and further optimizations can be done where needed.

The "Blueprint" - From "Idea" to "Specification" What’s better than a 100-page spec document? A spec you can actually run. Every chip starts as an "Idea." But to become a product, that idea must be evaluated (market fit, technical feasibility) and turned into a solid "Specification." This spec defines features, power, performance, and cost (PPA). This is where Model-Based System Engineering (MBSE) shines. Instead of static documents, we build executable models that become the "single source of truth," allowing us to validate our assumptions. A "runnable" spec ensures all teams (HW, SW, and validation) are building to the same, validated plan before the expensive design work starts. Have you ever been caught by a vague or outdated spec? How could an "executable" model have helped?

My problem with "software engineering" is that, to me, "engineering" suggests an activity that's grounded in empirically tested predictive models that are built on known causal mechanisms. We don't just predict that e.g., a function will have a higher likely failure rate because it's more complex, we have a pretty good idea *why*, too. (The ratio in the state space of ways it can be wrong / ways it can be right increases, so the probability of it being wrong goes up - basically, we make the goal's mouth smaller.) And, TBF, most teams aren't even predicting it. They just write the code and judge it on "feels". I think we *aspire* to engineering, but we're a long way off.

Sometimes we stand stuck in front of a closed door, not noticing there’s no wall beside it. That’s where TRIZ can help IT engineers find a way forward. In the 1960s, engineers developed TRIZ - the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving. It was based on the analysis of ~40,000 patents. Over time, TRIZ has been adapted to solve non-material and abstract problems as well. Why is this relevant for IT? Because IT systems are engineering systems. Just like engines or power grids, they evolve according to certain principles and TRIZ provides a structured framework for creative problem-solving, derived from patterns in thousands of patents. It’s highly applicable to software architecture. TRIZ in Software Architecture Lets review typical scenario: monolith application refactoring without moving to microservices. TRIZ shows us there’s no wall beside it. The goal is to improve maintainability, scalability, and development speed without the distributed complexity. Here’s how TRIZ principles guide the solution: 1. Segmentation: break the monolith into a modular monolith with enforced boundaries. 2. Local Quality: allow each module to use the most appropriate technology for its specific job. 3. Preliminary Action: build a robust CI/CD pipeline and use feature toggles to make deployments safe and fast. 4. The Other Way Around: invert the database dependency. Give modules private databases and make them communicate via APIs, not direct SQL. 5. Dynamicity: implement internal circuit breakers and bulkheads so modules can handle failure gracefully without bringing down the whole system. The result? A "TRIZ-Refactored" monolith that is segmented, technologically flexible, resilient, and ready for independent development - all within a single deployable unit. TRIZ gives engineers a powerful lens to solve technical contradictions and evolve systems intelligently. It’s not just about mechanics; it’s about fundamental inventive principles. Altshuller Institute for TRIZ studies: https://www.aitriz.org/#TRIZ#SoftwareArchitecture#SoftwareEngineering#Refactoring#SystemDesign

Think of it as digital LEGO for engineers: drag, drop, and link your systems together to automate repetitive or time-sensitive tasks. Here’s a scenario that really sparked my imagination:🤔 Every day, fuel composition reports arrive from the lab via email. Right now, someone has to manually check if the new fuel mix might cause issues. (Such as HIGH HYDROGEN content of fuel produced in upstream units) But what if… 🔹 n8n automatically reads the incoming email, 🔹 Extracts key values (like H₂, CH₄, or sulfur content), 🔹 Sends them to a lightweight FHinfinity evaluation API (Burner Analyzer tool), 🔹 And if the simulation predicts high fuel pressure or combustion instability… 🔹 It instantly alerts the operations team: “⚠️ Warning: With today’s fuel mix, FHinfinity predicts fuel pressure will exceed safe limits. We analyzed billions of AI crawler requests to figure out how each crawler handles JavaScript rendering, assets, and other behavior. Here's what we found. — Search engine crawlers still outpace AI crawlers, but it's growing significantly. Last month GPTBot, Claude, AppleBot, and PerplexityBot combined account for nearly 1.3 billion fetches—a little over 28% of Googlebot's volume. — Results consistently show that none of the major AI crawlers currently render JavaScript. However, Google's Gemini and AppleBot enable full JavaScript rendering, which may push others to follow. — AI crawlers place different priorities on content types. While ChatGPT prioritizes HTML, Claude focuses heavily on images. This may indicate that crawlers are specializing or have yet to refine their content prioritization strategies. — Interestingly, AI crawlers seem to be inefficient. Over 30% of requests are hitting invalid URLs. This suggests a need for improved URL strategies to avoid unnecessary requests. — Read our full analysis and recommendations to optimize your site for AI crawlers or block them altogether. https://lnkd.in/gg6KmD5V

I'm sharing the process I used to create a new feature for my project Begonia.pro, my local SEO platform for non tech-savvy users, thanks to AI. I developed a crawler and a website audit tool focused on local SEO. It took me 3 days with AI. Without it, it would have easily taken me 10 days, especially since I was coding a crawler for the first time. It cost me ~$15 of AI credits within my IDE. Step 1: Brainstorming & Specs - AIs used: Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT5 - Time: 2h The tool's idea is simple: crawling websites looking for SEO best practices or errors, and provides recommendations. I used AIs to: - Brainstorm - Write the functional specs - Choose the technical libraries. - Think about the UX I identified 25 tests for the audit, split into 4 categories: - Visibility on Google - Performance - Content & Presentation - Trust & Credibility Step 2: Database - AI used: GPT5 - Time: < 1h I don't let the AI code directly; I prefer to validate a database schema first. Step 3: Design - AI used: Claude Sonnet 4.5 - Time: < 10 min Simple step: I already have another audit tool (for Google Business Profile). I wanted the AI to replicate the same design. I briefed the AI directly in my IDE. Stunning result. The AI copied the components and reproduced the interface identically. Step 4: AI Dev - AI used: Claude Sonnet 4.5 - Time: < 20 min The AI generated the crawler and all the audit tests at once... or so I thought. In reality, a good half of the tests were empty shells or very basic. But that's more my fault, as I hadn't gone into detail in the specs. In any case, I would have spent hours doing the same thing! Step 5: Verification, Debugging, and Improvements - AIs used: Claude Sonnet 4.5 and GPT5 - Time: 2 days This is where the bulk of the work is: verifying what the AI did, adding missing cases, and explaining how to implement the more complicated tests. I used GPT5 as a code reviewer. (It has a tendency to over-complicate things; I then ask Claude Sonnet 4.5 to implement a middle ground). I also had to manage everything the AI left out (translations, error handling, etc.). But I barely coded at all: I just wrote prompts telling the AI what to do. Conclusion - Using multiple AIs based on their strengths is a best practice I'm using more and more. - The time saved by using AI to create this feature is undeniable! - The main problem: the (lack of) memory of AIs. Claude Sonnet 4.5 quickly forgets what it coded before. You have to keep showing it the code you're talking about. I wonder if it's possible to improve this by having it document its actions? I'm open to your feedback and ideas for improving my process! PS: If you want to test the feature, the link is in the comments.

I used AI for something nefarious this week, and I'm pretty happy with myself, actually* I was chatting to someone about their client and how a previous agency had rebuilt a site, but completely ignored all of the SEO implications of the rebuild. The site design was dated, but it ranked incredibly well thanks to the content and the overall architecture. The new site came along, and yes it looks good—but it tanked in the rankings and business dropped off a cliff. The old agency has now gone bust, emails have stopped being answered, and the client has no way to get back their old content—or so they think. Thanks to some detective work, we've managed to find a copy of the old site which is being hosted by the old agency on an old server, which by some miracle hasn't been taken offline yet. The situation falls to me: we need to get all 600 pages of content back so we can work through it, re-upload it, and get the page rankings back up to scratch. Of course, we can't just download 600 pages by going directly into the server, so we need a more novel approach. I wanted to use Screaming Frog to crawl the website, but my license has expired and I didn't want to spend £200 on a new one for this one task. The obvious next step? Ask AI. My first question to Gemini was, "Can you scrape this website?" to which it replied no; it could scrape a single page for me, but it wouldn't be able to scrape the whole site. I then did a Google search and found some tools, but they would download the full code of the page rather than just the clean content, which would cause a headache in the long run. Back to Gemini I go—and ask it to build a scraping tool for me. Sure, I could learn how to do it and go back into the world of Python, but this is what I would call a very specific, short-lived project with no need for maintainability or security. It just needed to be a fire-and-forget piece of script that would crawl a whole website and drop the content into a CSV file for me. 30 seconds later—I've got the script. I run it (and of course it fails), but I can see the problems and after a quick tête-à-tête with Gemini, we got the script finished. Now it's downloading all of the pages, with clean content, ready to be re-inserted back into the main website. The whole process took around an hour to write the script, run it, and get the results across. The client was made up because they had their content back, and I was happy because it gave me a LinkedIn post to write this week. Sometimes, you need to take a step back from the hole you're trying to fill to make sure you don't knock the house down in the process; and sometimes the most sophisticated tool for a job is a 30-second script built by an LLM. *Of course not really—but we're all playing the game to get LinkedIn clicks, aren't we?

⚡ Think prompts are just “text in, output out”? ⁣ Without structure, you’re leaving accuracy and scale on the table.⁣ ⁣ Messy prompts create inconsistent outputs.⁣ Small errors ripple into wasted hours, poor results, and higher costs.⁣ ⁣ 𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐁𝐞𝐚𝐭𝐬 𝐆𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤?⁣ AI isn’t just about creativity. It’s about precision.⁣ Structured prompts → predictable outputs → reliable performance.⁣ ⁣ 𝐌𝐞𝐞𝐭 𝐉𝐒𝐎𝐍: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐝𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐒𝐦𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐞𝐫 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭𝐬⁣ JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) isn’t just for developers.⁣ It’s a clean, universal format that organizes instructions, parameters, and context—making your AI work smarter, not harder.⁣ ⁣ 𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐤 𝐢𝐧 𝐁𝐨𝐱𝐞𝐬: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐉𝐒𝐎𝐍 𝐌𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐬𝐞𝐭⁣ Think of JSON as labeled boxes:⁣ • 𝐊𝐞𝐲𝐬 = Categories of information. The “what” (e.g., "task", "tone", "audience")⁣ • 𝐕𝐚𝐥𝐮𝐞𝐬 = The details AI needs. The “how” (details the AI executes on)⁣ • 𝐍𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 = Complexity made simple (context inside context)⁣ ⁣ 𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐉𝐒𝐎𝐍 𝐖𝐢𝐧𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭 𝐄𝐧𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠?⁣ • Prompts become reusable templates.⁣ • Collaboration between tech + business teams improves.⁣ • Easy handoff from prototype to production pipelines.⁣ • Compatibility with most AI frameworks.⁣ ⁣ 𝐀 𝐒𝐢𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐄𝐱𝐚𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞: 𝐓𝐮𝐫𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐨𝐬 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐨 𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐲⁣ Instead of one giant text blob, a JSON prompt can look like:⁣ • "task": "summarize document"⁣ • "tone": "professional"⁣ • "length": "200 words"⁣ This transforms a vague request into machine-readable clarity.⁣ ⁣ 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐦𝐨𝐧 𝐏𝐢𝐭𝐟𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐀𝐯𝐨𝐢𝐝⁣ • Forgetting commas → broken prompts.⁣ • Over-nesting → too complex to debug.⁣ • Mixing natural language with instructions in one block.⁣ ⁣ 𝐓𝐨𝐨𝐥𝐬 𝐓𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐤𝐞 𝐉𝐒𝐎𝐍 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐄𝐚𝐬𝐲⁣ LangChain, Haystack, OpenAI’s API, Anthropic’s SDK—all embrace JSON as a standard.⁣ ⁣ 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐁𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐄𝐝𝐠𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭𝐬⁣ Structured prompts:⁣ • Cut iteration cycles in half.⁣ • Save analyst and dev hours.⁣ • Build audit trails for compliance.⁣ ⁣ Start small. Standardize keys. Build prompt libraries like you build APIs.⁣ As AI matures, JSON-based prompts will be as standard as REST calls in software.⁣ ⁣ From chaos to clarity, JSON is the missing piece in prompt engineering.⁣ ⁣ Have you tried organizing your AI prompts with JSON yet?⁣ ⁣ This related post adds another layer to the discussion.⁣ ⁣ https://lnkd.in/drX5V8wv⁣ 🔁 Repost if this helped you.⁣ ⁣#AI#PromptEngineering#JSON#ArtificialIntelligence#AIDevelopment#TechInnovation

From Local LLMs to Real-Time Agents: Ollama’s Web Upgrade Local models are fast, private, and affordable — but their knowledge is frozen at the moment of training. That’s why hallucinations and outdated answers are so common. What Ollama brings now Ollama adds a web layer that makes local models behave like agents with real-time information access. Key features: 🔎 Search API → structured results (title, URL, snippet) 📄 Fetch API → full-page content, not just previews ⚙️ Tool integration → models can call search/fetch themselves via function calling Why reasoning models matter Some models don’t just answer — they explain their thinking. Examples: DeepSeek R1, Qwen3, GPT-OSS, IBM Granite. Benefits: ✅ Higher accuracy in multi-step tasks ✅ Transparency — users can see the chain of thought ✅ Reliability for coding, workflows, and enterprise use cases Frameworks like ReAct (Reason + Act) and Tree-of-Thoughts show how reasoning + tool use = smarter AI agents. Search + Reasoning = Game Changer Together, they remove the two biggest weaknesses of LLMs: ❌ Stale knowledge → fixed by live web access ❌ Hallucinations → reduced through explicit reasoning & cross-checking sources The result: grounded, current, and explained answers. Whenever OpenAI releases something, it becomes an event, even if they release something as simple as a popcorn. And one such popcorn release was AgentKit. But after building 10,000+ agents for enterprise clients, I can tell you that whole narrative misses the point. Visual builders have a hidden cost nobody talks about: The cognitive tax. A simple workflow is fine. But a medium-complexity one can easily hit 50+ nodes. That’s 50 decisions, connectors, and conditions you have to hold in your head. If the person who built it leaves, good luck. You’re left trying to decode a spaghetti diagram. Here’s the breakdown: → Visual graphs are a UI for logic. → AI Agents are language for logic. You can explain, test, and even have another AI audit logic written in natural language. You can't do that with a screenshot of a graph. So, where do different tools fit? ✅ n8n & AgentKit: They shine for on-ramps and quick wins. Wiring up a few triggers, fetching data, and pushing to Slack? Perfect. They’re great for showing a simple idea to a stakeholder. But they get squeezed from both sides. Simple agents are getting better, and complex systems demand code. ✅ LangGraph (from LangChain): Excellent for developers who want code-first, explicit control over complex agent graphs. If you live in Python/TypeScript and need precision, it’s a solid choice. The challenge? Getting from a cool prototype to a governed, production-ready system is where most agent projects die. This is the gap we built Lyzr AI to fill. Lyzr is the production bridge. It’s designed for the entire Agent Development Lifecycle (ADLC) - from idea to enterprise-grade service, without rewrites. Here's how Lyzr is different: 🔹 Visuals Where They Help, Code Where It Counts Inspect agent behavior visually, then flip a switch to dev mode and go straight to code. The two stay in sync. No work is thrown away as complexity grows. 🔹 Built-in Evals & Guardrails Promote agents based on metrics, not vibes. Responsible AI policies, hallucination checks, and PII guardrails are baked in, not bolted on. 🔹 Model Freedom AgentKit locks you into OpenAI. Lyzr is model-agnostic. Swap GPTs, Claude, Gemini, or local LLMs based on cost, latency, or privacy needs. 🔹 Run Anywhere Deploy in your own cloud (VPC) or on-prem. Your secrets and data never leave your boundary. This is non-negotiable for enterprises. Here’s the final scorecard: n8n: Keep it for simple, legible automations. AgentKit: A smooth on-ramp if you're all-in on OpenAI. LangGraph: The go-to for code-level control of agent logic. Lyzr: The full-stack platform to get agents into production - with the governance, evals, and observability enterprises actually need. OpenAI is great at winning PLG battles. But enterprises don’t buy stunts; they buy systems.#AI#AgentKit#n8n#OpenAI#LangChain#Lyzr#Automation#EnterpriseAI#LLMs

I spent some time in the network logs of OpenAI's new Atlas browser, and there’s a fundamental technical shift here that marketers need to understand: First, the basics: Atlas is a browser where the ChatGPT is the default interface. It's built to answer questions and complete tasks directly, without necessarily sending you to a URL. Now the strategy: most marketers' first reaction will be that this just means 'fewer clicks.' I think that misses the real story, which you can only see in the logs. The difference isn't just what Atlas returns; it's how it gets the answer. Classic ChatGPT works like a souped-up search engine. It runs a series queries, scrapes the snippets, and summarizes them. It's entirely dependent on what a search crawler has already indexed. Atlas is a fundamentally different machine. It's an agent and it can see and interact with everything (even JS). It's executing DOM clicks on size selectors, dismissing modals, and scrolling through dynamic product grid. It's interacting with the live, JavaScript-rendered front end. But I think this isn't just about Atlas. This is a clear preview of where Google AI Mode is headed, and a peek into the future of the SERP: led by chat with blue links as secondary information. The user adoption of Atlas itself doesn't matter bc this is the beginning of chat-first browsing and 'agent experience' as an entire pillar of the web. Winning in this new environment will come down to agent readiness: Think about how Atlas resolves a query like "waterproof trail shoes under $150 delivered by Friday." It isn't sitting around matching keywords, it's executing a task against your live site. If your JavaScript is slow, or your checkout flow is clunky, the agent will fail. Let's be honest: most brands' front ends are a mess of JavaScript and clunky user flows, many sites will inadvertently block Atlas' bot, and their design is optimized for humans who know how to get around the web, not for an impatient agent. That's the entire opportunity. The advantage goes to the companies who do the unglamorous work of making their sites fast, clean, and scriptable. These are the strategies I'll be watching: - Optimizing for agent usability, not just crawler indexability. This is the moment when a fast, scriptable, client-side rendered front end becomes a major competitive advantage. - Treating your website like an API for agents. Every interactive element is a chokepoint that must be fast and reliable. - Doubling down on structured data. Structured data will be the ground truth an agent can use to validate the information it finds on your live site. At its core, this is about shifting from optimizing for discovery to optimizing for action. The game is no longer just about being found; it's about being usable by an AI. Atlas is live @ chatgpt.com/atlas Spend an hour looking at how it actually interacts with sites in your vertical. Happy optimizing 👀

Quick Tip for Jetpack Compose Developers 💡 Stop passing ViewModel instances directly into your composable functions. 🚫 The Anti-Pattern: fun MyComposable(viewModel: MyViewModel) ✅ The Best Practice (State Hoisting): fun MyComposable(uiState: MyUiState, onDoSomething: () -> Unit) Why? As the official documentation warns, ViewModels are not part of the Composition. Passing them down can tightly couple your UI to the data source and lead to potential memory leaks, as the composable might hold a reference it shouldn't. By passing only the state data and lambdas for events, you create composables that are: Easier to test in isolation. Highly reusable. Less prone to lifecycle-related bugs. Do you follow this pattern strictly in your code?#JetpackCompose#AndroidDev#Kotlin#MobileDevelopment#SoftwareArchitecture#Android

"Agentic commerce" elicits different reactions depending on who you ask. Whether you're an excited shopper or a concerned customer, find out how Adyen is helping merchants prepare for the next era of AI-driven shopping in our latest blog post
Ingo Uytdehaage
co-CEO at Adyen
5mo Edited
Agentic commerce is fragmenting before it scales. Our role is clear: to ensure merchants stay in control as AI begins to transact on behalf of consumers. For shoppers, this new form of commerce is effortless. For our customers, it raises real questions. What happens to your brand when the purchase happens inside a chat interface? Who owns the customer relationship? And how do you maintain control over payments, risk, and data when the AI agent acts as the middle layer? Across the ecosystem, leaders like Google, Visa, Mastercard, and OpenAI are building the protocols that will power these AI-driven shopping experiences. We’re actively collaborating with them, bringing the perspective of some of the world’s largest enterprises to the table to ensure these new standards reflect their needs. That’s the idea behind our merchant-first foundation for agentic commerce: an open framework that keeps merchants in command of payments, data, and risk, no matter which protocol or platform the shopper’s agent uses. AI will redefine how people shop. Our job is to make sure it does so on our customers’ terms: with control, transparency, and trust built in from day one. https://lnkd.in/ekx_YrHp

# Guillermo Rauch’s Post
Vercel•58K followers
2y
Yesterday we introduced the AI SDK 3.0 with Generative UI support. In 2023, Casetext was acquired for $650M because they "removed" hallucinations from their platform, through eval-driven development. When LLMs came out, Casetext had already existed for 10 years. But they quickly pivoted into GenAI and found a solid use case in legal analysis. The use case worked because lawyers and paralegals are drowning in work and documents. (One of the reasons so much money is being poured into legal-tech currently). However, they also don't want their license and practice to be in jeopardy if an AI they use hallucinates facts and citations. What Casetext figured out so early on, and what is only now gaining traction, is something called eval-driven development. The paradigm itself is not new, it's in fact similar to test-driven development in software engineering. Here's what you do 1/ Break down a multi-step workflow into a series of micro steps. 2/ Build lots and lots of evals (i.e. input prompt & output pairs) for each micro step. 3/ Improve prompts and models until all evals for each micro step pass. As a result, your rate of hallucinations goes down considerably. And that is what justified the sky high valuation of Casetext.

In a conversation with Josh Twist, Co-founder & CEO of Zuplo, Guy Podjarny(Founder and CEO of Tessl) shared why he believes software development is shifting from code-first to spec-first. The problem: AI agents are powerful but unreliable. They hallucinate APIs, forget past decisions, and struggle with repetition. The solution: define software through specifications. By capturing intent, constraints, and requirements as specs, agents can generate consistent, adaptable code. Guy outlined three stages of this journey: 1️⃣ Spec-Assisted: agents consume structured knowledge. 2️⃣ Spec-Driven: captures critical definitions, specs become the source of truth. 3️⃣ Spec-Centric: code is disposable, specs endure. 💡 I’m convinced that the future of enterprise AI isn’t about more data, it’s about a new engineering paradigm - Agent Development Kits (ADKs) by Google. Traditional monolithic stacks tie every decision to a single model or a set of pipelines. That’s fine for a few use cases, but it stalls innovation when a business needs to loop in external APIs, maintain state across sessions, or run code on the fly. ADK flips that model on its head. Because the ADK’s primitives - Agent, Tool, Callback, Session, Memory, Artifact, Planner - are all first‑class objects, you can compose workflows that evolve, audit, and self‑optimize. It’s not just about faster deployment; it’s about a new kind of agility that lets product teams iterate on business logic as fast as they iterate on code. Key pieces you’ll see in any ADK stack: • Agents: task‑specific workers powered by LLMs or deterministic workflows. • Tools: plug‑in APIs, code runners, search engines. • Callbacks: hooks for logging, safety, or custom logic. • Session & State: short‑term context for each dialogue. • Memory: long‑term user insights. • Artifact: persistent files or media. • Planner: ReAct‑style step‑wise reasoning. In practice, companies that have adopted ADK report up to 4× faster prototype cycles, a 30% reduction in deployment costs, and a 20% improvement in compliance audit scores - because every interaction is logged, auditable, and tweakable. Are we ready to shift from building single‑purpose models to designing modular agent ecosystems? What would the first agent you’d build look like, and how would it change your organization?#AI#AgentDevelopment#LowCodeAI#Innovation#TechLeadership

2y
It's incredible to reflect on what the Next.js team at Vercel has accomplished in 2023. 1️⃣ First, when you read "framework", these days it actually means compiler and build system The level of automation and optimization Next.js brings to the table is unparalleled. You get a simple DX, the compiler takes on all the complexity. The team has made so much progress this year on our new Rust-based compiler, which is a step-function improvement. A lazy & incremental compiler brings a new level of scale and DX for 10x-100x bigger apps. 2️⃣ Partial Pre-rendering, RSC, and Streaming are an entirely new foundation Very few companies (arguably just Google and Meta) have had a comparable tech stack, and even though React is OSS, the surrounding framework was not. Next.js enables you to build and deploy software that rivals the quality and scale of the tech giants. Speaking of Black Friday, Next.js specially evens the playing field of e-commerce. 3️⃣ Next.js is set up to power the future of software ChatGPT, Perplexity, Pi, Midjourney, Leonardo.ai, Humata.ai, Claude, Phind, Julius.ai, Gamma, You, and many other incredible AI products are powered by Next.js. Querying an LLM & other AI models, and then server-rendering and streaming the resulting UI will be a big part of how the future of software is built. Next.js is just getting started. And I would be remiss if I didn't suggest to check out the all-new nextjs.org/learn we just shipped!

# Guillermo Rauch’s Post
1y
⛈︎ The call is coming from inside the cloud The (D)DoS attack landscape of the internet has changed dramatically over the past few years. A lot of attacks on Vercel customers are increasingly coming from within the hyperscalers. Just yesterday we blocked a targeted distributed attack peaking at 1,000s+ of concurrent IPs. We suspect at least 17,949 machines were compromised. A few interesting takeaways: • The clouds are not keeping up. This specific attack pattern has been recurring and the resources they control are significant. • You can't bank on IP reputation signals. These IPs are very high reputation. Vetted customers have been compromised and their machines are being mis-used. We've had to develop very surgical mitigations to prevent impacting legitimate traffic coming from the same ASNs. • Security is the core advantage of managed services. The most likely culprit here is boxes running outdated software in a VM or VPS with manually configured SSH access and networking getting breached. From the provider's perspective, these machines are a black box. It's hard to understand if they're secure, it's hard to help the customer, and it's hard to understand if their traffic patterns are malicious. tl;DR: you can sleep soundly at night that Vercel is both providing you a more secure compute primitive, while also protecting you from these nasty attacks out of the box
Hearing this makes me wonder what more cloud providers could do to identify malicious usage of resources and stop it at the source. One of the first applications I deployed to the cloud after my bootcamp was hit by a DDoS attack and taken down. I shortly redeployed, adding rate-limiters and didn't have any further issues. It's scary to think about how much attackers have evolved since then and how they are leveraging the scalability of the cloud to do harm. More recently, I've been deploying a lot of applications to Vercel, and I've never faced similar issues, despite these advances in attack patterns. Keep up the great work!
I think AI can play a key role in cybersecurity by identifying malicious patterns faster than humans, reducing the effectiveness of DDoS attacks and minimizing potential downtime

Powerful#DDOS botnet is upon us requiring new defenses! https://lnkd.in/ePt-pXeu As Brian Krebs details in his article, this botnet has unleashed record-breaking DDoS attacks including the hyper-volumetric tens of Tbps (e.g. 30+) So what's going on? Summary: Fact One: Attacks are faster than their Mean-time-to-Resolution (MTTR) Most DDoS Solutions require some time to detect and even more time to put in place a mitigation strategy - - this attack changes the game! These hyper-volumetric attacks are very short lived, lasting 30-70 seconds. A rapid concentration of force, trying to avoid detection — leaving no time for human intervention or even automated activation of On-Demand services. If unmitigated, the network and application failures can last far beyond the duration of the attack. Fact Two: Complicated Attack vectors: These attacks are not simple floods and cannot be mitigated by many simple solutions such as firewall ACLs or human filtering rules. These attacks are predominantly Distributed UDP Carpet Bombing attacks that require intelligent detection and mitigation systems that go beyond simple port or IP blocking. Fact Three: You get what you pay for - Many Companies Suffer w/ Old Solutions As Brian Krebs reported (https://lnkd.in/dHHK93Yc), Aisuru has already disrupted U.S. ISPs and enterprises, demonstrating just how devastating these hyper-volumetric attacks can be. Fact Four: Review / Audit your DDOS solution today! Hyper-volumetric DDoS attacks of this scale aimed at critical infrastructure, ISPs, and any other organization, during peak hours could potentially cripple them Fact Five: "On Demand Cloud DDoS Solutions, especially with Netflow Detection are dead solutions" Organizations relying on “On-Demand DDoS Products” or services, or Flow-detection based Legacy L3/4 DDoS Scrubbing Center solutions are advised to rethink their defense strategy given the new threat landscape. Many of the attacks are so short-lived, that there is no time for human intervention or even automatic activation of On-Demand services.

The cybersecurity industry has an asymmetry problem. Attackers launch AI-driven exploits for less than a $1 and move at machine speed. Defenders spend $4.88M per breach and weeks coordinating humans. Detection and response—no matter how fast—can't win a race where the other side chooses when it starts. That's why we built LayerV. We make critical infrastructure invisible before attacks happen. Not hidden behind firewalls. Not obscured by access controls. Cryptographically unlinkable at the network layer. Your servers don't exist to scanners, can't be enumerated in breaches, and disappear from the attack surface entirely. We authenticate identity before network packets reach your infrastructure. If an attacker can't see you, exploitation becomes impossible. This is how the internet should have worked from the beginning. We're lead authors of OpenNHP (Open Network Hiding Protocol), working with the Cloud Security Alliance to turn infrastructure invisibility into an internet standard. Our approach to trusted digital identity was presented at the United Nations earlier this year. Founded by security architects from CrowdStrike, Cisco, Microsoft, and Disney, we've spent 25 years building defenses at every layer. We know why they fail. Defense-in-depth assumes attackers are already inside—we're building so they never get a foothold. If you're tired of playing catch-up, it's time for a different approach.

# Guillermo Rauch’s Post
[![View profile for Guillermo Rauch, graphic]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rauchg?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Guillermo Rauch](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rauchg?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
CEO at Vercel
3mo
The internet was abuzz last week with McMaster-Carr's website being such a great example of performance. Which it is: check it out for yourself at[mcmaster.com](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://mcmaster.com&urlhash=8SoW&trk=public_post-text)So, our community set out to recreate it using[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text). In just a few hours, NextMaster was born. With the help of AI and[https://v0.dev](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://v0.dev&urlhash=uj6f&trk=public_post-text),[Ethan Niser](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ethanniser?trk=public_post-text)had a prototype up and running. And then over the weekend,[Rhys Sullivan](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rhyssullivan?trk=public_post-text)and Arman Kumaraswamy perfected it.
NextMaster is open source and a great learning resource on the latest and greatest[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)capabilities:
•Partial Prerendering, which makes all loads snappy
•Server-side Streaming, which helps personalize the page without client side JS and performance penalties. This is used for logged-in states and carts.
•Prefetching, which makes all the client side loads really snappy. •Image optimization
What's more, we set out to demonstrate the scale of[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text). McMaster-Carr features an impressive 700,000+ product catalog. We used[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text)'s batch inference capabilities to generate 1,000,000 imagined products, complete with titles and descriptions.
In short, this app \*feels\* like a static or CDN-cached website, but it's in reality a fully dynamic, server-rendered experience. While CDNs got us here, the future is in hybrid experiences that don't de-opt to expensive and slow client-side JS rendering.
This is a blueprint, I believe, for the future of e-commerce on the web. I just want to highlight this uses the `onmousedown`. If only that had been the default behavior of onclick, humans collectively would've saved a nearly inconceivable amount of time.
Consider there's 8 billion humans on the planet, and each had clicked a <button onclick=...> once today. They lose say 100 milliseconds each time without onmousedown. Well that's 800 million seconds. Which is 13 million minutes, which is 500 thousand hours, which is 9,259 days, which 25 years of human time lost!
This started as a dumb thought, but I don't think we often consider how much time humans lose waiting for computers. I can't comment on this specific implementation without more[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_comment-text)experience, but I do appreciate how much concern Vercel puts into performance. While the popularity of web-first approaches have inevitably brought challenges and growing pains, it usually feels as though software performance has not scaled with hardware at all. I suppose it's partly because "There are two ways of constructing a software design: One way is to make it so simple that there are obviously no deficiencies, and the other way is to make it so complicated that there are no obvious deficiencies." The former is performant yet difficult to arrive at.

If you are in anyway interested in web performance, read the post below. Insane.
[![View profile for Guillermo Rauch, graphic]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rauchg?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-image)
[Guillermo Rauch](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rauchg?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-name)

3mo
The internet was abuzz last week with McMaster-Carr's website being such a great example of performance. Which it is: check it out for yourself at[mcmaster.com](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://mcmaster.com&urlhash=8SoW&trk=public_post_reshare-text)So, our community set out to recreate it using[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_reshare-text). In just a few hours, NextMaster was born. With the help of AI and[https://v0.dev](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://v0.dev&urlhash=uj6f&trk=public_post_reshare-text),[Ethan Niser](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ethanniser?trk=public_post_reshare-text)had a prototype up and running. And then over the weekend,[Rhys Sullivan](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rhyssullivan?trk=public_post_reshare-text)and Arman Kumaraswamy perfected it.
NextMaster is open source and a great learning resource on the latest and greatest[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_reshare-text)capabilities:
•Partial Prerendering, which makes all loads snappy
•Server-side Streaming, which helps personalize the page without client side JS and performance penalties. This is used for logged-in states and carts.
•Prefetching, which makes all the client side loads really snappy. •Image optimization
What's more, we set out to demonstrate the scale of[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post_reshare-text). McMaster-Carr features an impressive 700,000+ product catalog. We used[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post_reshare-text)'s batch inference capabilities to generate 1,000,000 imagined products, complete with titles and descriptions.
In short, this app \*feels\* like a static or CDN-cached website, but it's in reality a fully dynamic, server-rendered experience. While CDNs got us here, the future is in hybrid experiences that don't de-opt to expensive and slow client-side JS rendering.
This is a blueprint, I believe, for the future of e-commerce on the web. You can deploy your own to[Vercel](https://www.linkedin.com/company/vercel?trk=public_post_reshare-text), or head here and just click around to experience the speed:[https://lnkd.in/g3hXg-gx](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/g3hXg-gx&urlhash=-5ts&trk=public_post_reshare-text)

I'm having a hard time wrapping my head around how it's possible to have a dynamic site be this ridiculously performant. Pre-rendered, static sites used to be the only way, and they still somehow weren't this fast.
Browsing this site feels like it anticipates your next move (like a magical prerender hint) and the next page seemingly loads before your onmouseup completes (semi-nerd moment there). Now, what this creates is an exceptionally-chatty solution, as this anticipation is driven by pre-fetching when you hover (which creates that concept of anticipation as it's essentially fetching in case you decide to click). So hundreds upon hundreds of requests, but when you're optimized for caching and running on edge computing, who cares?
Either way, extremely impressive work. Can't wait to see this on my favorite, real sites!

I recently heard that OpenAI switched their[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)app to Remix. I’ve come across Remix before and have heard some good things about it. Naturally, I started wondering why they made the switch. There are great blog posts and YouTube videos explaining their reasoning, like this one:[https://lnkd.in/egza-9wT](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/egza-9wT&urlhash=5mix&trk=public_post-text)Lately, I’ve been frustrated with the developer experience (DX) on my current stack—it’s been a constant battle with npm errors, package conflicts, and dependency issues. I even spent a few days diving into TanStack Router, React Router 7 that is actually a new Remix. At this point, I wasn’t sure whether to keep pushing forward or try something else entirely. However, I decided to give[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)15 a shot (after multiple attempts to upgrade from[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)14). It wasn’t easy in the middle of an ongoing project with countless dependencies, but I finally managed to make the switch—alongside alpha versions of other libraries that support React 19.
I have to say,[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)15 feels different. It’s snappier and seems more promising, especially when it comes to developer experience. For now, I’m sticking with it, as my initial impression is that the DX has definitely improved.

News Alert: OpenAI Switches from Next.JS to Remix!
OpenAI has recently made a notable shift in their technology stack, moving from Next.JS to Remix for their web projects. While Next.JS remains a powerful and widely used framework, OpenAI’s transition highlights a strategic preference for Remix’s advanced capabilities in web performance and routing.
Here’s why OpenAI switched to Remix:
1. Improved Server-Side Rendering (SSR): Remix’s SSR enables more efficient, dynamic data loading, ideal for real-time applications.
2. Flexible Routing: Remix excels in handling nested routes, simplifying the development and maintenance of complex applications.
3. Performance Optimization: With built-in prefetching, Remix ensures faster load times and a smoother user experience.
4. Unified Data Management: Remix simplifies data handling by integrating server and client-side management, reducing complexity and boosting performance.
Note: Next.JS remains a robust, versatile framework widely favored for static and server-side rendered applications. Both Remix and Next.JS have unique strengths, and the choice depends on the specific needs of each project.
What’s your take? Do you think Remix will become the go-to framework for companies, or is Next.JS still your preferred choice? Share your thoughts on which framework suits different types of projects and It's interesting to know why OpenAI moved from[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)to Remix.
OpenAI recently shifted from[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)to Remix, driven by Remix’s performance, enhanced developer experience, and specific capabilities that align with OpenAI's needs:
𝟭) 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗲𝗿-𝗖𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰 𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗘𝗻𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁: Remix focuses on server-side rendering (SSR) and progressive enhancement, making it easier to build fast, SEO-friendly applications that prioritise server logic. This aligns with OpenAI’s focus on performance and efficient server-side management.
𝟮) 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗺𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗟𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴: Remix offers powerful data-loading patterns that handle nested routes and layouts efficiently. It optimises data fetching, making apps faster and more responsive by reducing client-side complexity.
𝟯) 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗢𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀: Remix eliminates unnecessary JavaScript, optimises assets, and leverages modern web standards. This approach reduces page load times and improves user experience, which is crucial for data-heavy platforms like OpenAI.
𝟰) 𝗙𝗼𝗰𝘂𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗪𝗲𝗯𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘀: Remix prioritises standard web APIs, creating accessible, resilient, and performant web apps. This approach aligns well with the needs of modern web applications.
𝟱) 𝗘𝗻𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗱𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲: Remix’s features like route-based code splitting, improved error handling, and intuitive nested routing provide a more productive workflow compared to[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text).
𝟲) 𝗙𝗹𝗲𝘅𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗥𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗡𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗱𝗟𝗮𝘆𝗼𝘂𝘁𝘀: Remix’s routing system allows for intuitive and efficient layout and route definition, ideal for complex applications with nested UI structures.
𝟳) 𝗥𝗼𝗯𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗘𝗱𝗴𝗲𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴: Remix offers better fallback mechanisms for network issues and errors, enhancing overall app resilience and user experience.
OpenAI's move to Remix reflects a focus on performance, streamlined data handling, and a superior developer experience tailored for high-performance web applications.[#RemixRun](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/remixrun&trk=public_post-text)[#Nextjs](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/nextjs&trk=public_post-text)[#WebDevelopment](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/webdevelopment&trk=public_post-text)[#SSR](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/ssr&trk=public_post-text)[#PerformanceOptimisation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/performanceoptimisation&trk=public_post-text)[#WebStandards](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/webstandards&trk=public_post-text)[#DeveloperExperience](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/developerexperience&trk=public_post-text)[#OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/openai&trk=public_post-text)[#TechInnovation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/techinnovation&trk=public_post-text)[#FrontendDevelopment](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/frontenddevelopment&trk=public_post-text)

🎉Hey Everyone! Super excited to drop the deets on my latest experiment: 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗧𝗿𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗖𝗼-𝗣𝗶𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗔𝗽𝗽. 🛠️Tech Talk
Recently, I've been playing around with LangChain, OpenAI &[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text), and have spun up something awesome with CoPilot-Kit [it's an open-source toolkit for crafting custom AI Copilots 🤖]. But get this: it's not just any Gen AI chatbot—it's an App-aware AI. Chat with it, and it updates app states, does your research, and auto-refreshes your itineraries. Basically It can see the current app state + take action inside your app, Cool right?
🙌Jump In!
It's all open-source! I think it's a nifty little project for getting your feet wet with[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text)and Its a kind of Hello World app to understand the whole new world of Lanchain/AI based applications. Whether you wanna squash bugs, drop new features, or just tinker with the code, there's space for you.
👨💻Why Join?
- Building smart apps is a blast
- Gain some legit coding chops
- Mess with TypeScript and[Next.js](http://Next.js?trk=public_post-text), OpenAI capabilities. 🚀Grab the code, dive in, and start pushing updates.
Hit that star ⭐on the repo if you dig it, and consider forking it to spin up your own cool tweaks.

# No Priors: Artificial Intelligence | Technology | Startups - AI Superpowers for Frontend Developers, with Vercel Founder/CEO Guillermo Rauch
Episode Date: August 31, 2023
Starting point is 00:00:00 So much of the web runs on Vercell, and now it'll run on Vercell and AI. Elad and I are super excited to welcome Guillermo Rouch, the founder and CEO of Vercell. It's one of the most popular developer-fund-and-framework companies and is widely used by Adobe, Octa, eBay, and others. We unpack the company's AI strategy, what's next for the web and more. Guillermo, welcome to NoPriars. Thank you. I'm excited to be here. Just for people who are not super familiar with Versailles, can you give us a quick explanation of the company? Yeah, you described it well. We're basically a web infrastructure company. We provide the frameworks, tools, infrastructure, and workflows for companies to deploy the most dynamic and ambitious websites on the internet.
Starting point is 00:00:48 So we power anything from the technology behind chat GPD, in fact, is powered by Next.js, our open source framework, to websites like Underarmor.com or Nintendo. though, where we provide the infrastructure to serve all their traffic and help them iterate on their web presence. And what's the sort of founding story of this? Technically, I started it at the very, very end of 2015, but I kind of settled on the idea and launched some of my first prototypes at the beginning of 2016. Yeah, it's hard to imagine even not existing now. At the time, what drove your belief that this was a different defensible product than the incumbent
Starting point is 00:01:27 clouds? So there's an interesting duality in me. On one hand, I'm basically a missionary of the web. I want the web to win. I want open platforms to win. I want developers to win. On the other hand, I really love Apple and companies that invest a lot in design and integration and making things really easy. So in many ways, the inspiration was, can we create a developer platform that does for the cloud what maybe like the iPhone did or the MacBook did for personal computing. And at the time, I had just sold my company to Automatic, the company behind the WordPress. So I had this idea in my mind of just making it really, really easy for developers to deploy an idea to the global web. And to start focusing on the front end, which is sort of my strength. I've been a front engineer for the vast majority of my life. there's always been sort of this, you know, almost like disdain in engineering for Fron
Starting point is 00:02:31 and it's like the last thing you worry about. But we've kind of turned that upside down and we've made the case that Fronon is the most important thing that your company has because that's where you meet your customer. That's where you can accelerate your ...

Dan Shipper is the CEO and cofounder of Every. Every week he explores the frontiers of AI in his column, Chain of Thought, and on his podcast, ‘AI & I.’
# Vercel’s Guillermo Rauch on What Comes After Coding
As AI writes code, developers must become better product thinkers
February 4, 2025 · Updated February 21, 2026
TL;DR: Today we’re releasing a new episode of our podcast AI & I. I go in depth with Guillermo Rauch, the cofounder and CEO of Vercel, a cloud platform for hosting and deploying web applications. We get into how AI creates new levels of abstraction in coding, his take on the allocation economy, and why specialized agents are the future. Watch on X or YouTube, or listen on Spotify or Apple Podcasts. Here’s a link to the episode transcript.
Guillermo Rauch’s code is everywhere.
OpenAI’s website is hosted on his company’s platform, Perplexity’s real-time chat feature is powered by his open-source tool, and if you’re building a new website or coding real-time features—like live notifications or instant messages—there’s a good chance you’ll use his code too.
Guillermo has been coding since he was 10 years old. He’s now the cofounder and CEO of Vercel, a platform that helps developers build fast, personalized web experiences. He’s also the creator of open-source tools like Next.js and Socket.IO.
The thing is, he doesn’t think of himself as a coder.
“I don't think I would identify… as a coder, even though that’s what I obsessed about for years,” he says, sitting opposite me wearing a black sweater, groomed moustache, and wry grin.
A successful software business is about a lot more than just code, he says; it’s about having the drive to build a practical, useful product. “Coding is a specific skill, and when things are specific skills, machines tend to take them over time,” he says, “so what I try to separate is, what are the meta skills… not as easily replicated by machines that you should still nurture?” Meta skills, Guillermo believes, “tend to be more around very high-level conceptual thinking.”
In a world where AI can handle routine coding work, Guillermo has noticed developers at Vercel becoming more full stack. “I think it’s an important asset to have… with [Vercel’s AI coding copilot] v0 for example, they can do design. They can bring context, data, copywriting into their creations that otherwise would have required chatting with other people and crowdsourcing ideas.
“The trend has been away from the implementation detail, which is the code, and toward the end goal, which is to deliver a great product or a great experience.”
I spent a fascinating hour talking with Guillermo. The whole conversation is riveting, and you can check it out here:
If you want a quick summary, here are some of the themes we touched on related to building great products and lasting companies:
Create a free account to continue reading

Vercel’s Guillermo Rauch: Building the Generative Web with AI | Sequoia Capital
# Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch: Building the Generative Web with AI
Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch has spent years obsessing over reducing the friction between having an idea and getting it online. Now with AI, he’s achieving something even more ambitious: making software creation accessible to anyone with a keyboard. Guillermo explains how v0 has grown to 3 million users by focusing on reliability and quality, why ChatGPT has become their fastest-growing customer acquisition channel, and how AI is enabling “virtual coworkers” across design, development, and marketing. He shares his contrarian view that the future belongs to ephemeral, generated-on-demand applications rather than traditional installed software, and why he believes we’re on the cusp of the biggest transformation to the web in its history.
Vercel Founder and CEO Guillermo Rauch, who led the creation of Next.js, explains how generative AI is redefining every layer of software creation. His insights spotlight the massive opportunities and emerging challenges for founders in a world where anyone can build, and software is generated, not just written.
AI products have built-in success metrics: Unlike traditional software where founders struggle to define meaningful metrics, AI products come with natural feedback loops. Code acceptance rates, deployment success, and user engagement provide immediate signals for product improvement—creating a built-in compass for iterating toward better outcomes.
Quality and reliability are non-negotiable for AI tools: The gap between demo and production-ready AI lies in reliability. Users of AI coding tools have zero patience for errors—if something doesn’t work immediately, they abandon it. Invest heavily in fine-tuning, custom model training and quality controls to ensure consistent, production-grade outputs.
Expand your builder funnel beyond traditional developers: The biggest opportunity isn’t just making existing developers more productive—it’s enabling designers, marketers and anyone with ideas to create functional applications through natural language. This represents a fundamental expansion from millions of traditional developers to hundreds of millions of potential creators.
Design for agents as first-class users: Your future customers may be AI agents as much as humans. APIs, runtimes and frameworks need to be agent-friendly, supporting both synchronous and asynchronous workflows. Think about how your product serves as a platform for virtual “coworkers” and how agentic interfaces change user onboarding.
Build self-improving, ephemeral experiences: The web is evolving from dynamic to generative, with applications created just-in-time for each user. Guillermo advises designing for composability and instant onboarding rather than permanent installation. The future belongs to ephemeral, personalized software generated on-demand rather than built once and distributed broadly....

Interview: Vercel’s Guillermo Rauch talks up AI-generated UI, unpacks Next.js relationship to React
Talking to Dev Class at the AWS re:Invent conference in Las Vegas, Vercel’s CEO Guillermo Rauch says he’s all-in on AI, the recurrent theme at the event. “I think AI is going to get embedded into every application,” he tells us.
The big new thing at Vercel is V0 (pronounced Vee Zero). “With V0 you just write in English what you want, and we create it for you. And that gives you your first version of the product. We just announced yesterday, vision support. So with vision support you can draw a sketch of your app or place a screenshot, and then we’ll create a UI that matches that. We don’t just copy pixel by pixel. We’re actually giving you an accessible version, we give you a professional UI. We’re doing our best to give you production-grade code,” Rauch says.
V0 designs can be amended by further text input, so like any other type of design it is an iterative process. It works by generating code using React, Tailwind CSS and shadcn/ui.
Is Vercel now competing with Figma, the web-based design tool which Adobe intends to acquire, if it can clear regulatory hurdles? “I don’t think we’re competing directly. I think we’re rethinking the approach,” says Rauch, who is also the inventor of the Next.js framework. “You can still take a screenshot from your Figma document, paste it into V0, and get a real-world implementation. What I think is going to happen is that a lot of people are going to go directly to V0, and perhaps skip some of those processes when it was not necessary.”
The appeal of V0 is the idea of reducing the drudgery of building everyday designs. Another aspect though is to end the notion of designers coming up with something that looks good, but which when handed over to developers proves difficult to implement. “We’re trying to make it such that you never again create an impossible design. From the get-go, you’re working on the surface that you’re actually deploying to,” he adds.
Next.js is a popular framework though a common complaint is that “Next.js is difficult to deploy anywhere but Vercel.” Rauch says that “the fact that for example, [AWS] Amplify just announced latest Next.js support, is evidence that Next.js is actually self-hostable. What sometimes gets confused is what is the responsibility of the application framework, and what is the responsibility of the infrastructure provider? AWS is not open source, and neither is Vercel. So I think developers would like to see better documentation of where one starts and ends, and we just improved our documentation for that on the Next.js website.”
Vercel, he tells us, is “an infrastructure services company,” and some new features depend on those services – though they are not necessarily exclusive to Next.js. “I’ll give you an example. We just announced that feature called partial pre-rendering” This is an experimental feature in Next.js 14. “What partial pre rendering does is that it marr...

Corey: It’s true, if I were talking to someone who looked at what I’ve done, and for some Godforsaken reason, they wanted to follow in my footsteps, well, that path has been closed, so learning a bunch of Perl early on and translating it all to bad bash scripts and the rest, and then maybe picking up Python isn’t really the way that I would advise someone getting started today. The de facto lingua franca of the internet is JavaScript, whether we like it or not, and I would strongly suggest that be someone’s first language despite the fact that I’m bad at it, I don’t understand it, and therefore it makes me angry.
Guillermo: Yeah, it’s so funny because it sounds like my story. And my personal journey was, when I was a kid, I had a—I knew I wanted to hack around with computers, and reverse engineer them, and improve them, and just create my own things, and I had these options for what programming language I could go with. And I tried it all: PHP, Perl, [Mod PHP 00:02:12], [Mod Perl 00:02:13], Apache, LAMP, cgi-bin folders, all the whole nine yards. And regardless of what back-end technology I used, I encountered this striking fact, which was… the thing that can make your product really stand out in a web browser is typically involving JavaScript in some fashion. So, when Google came out with suggestions as you would type in a search box, my young kid Argentinian brain blew up. I was like, “Holy crap, they can suggest, they can read my mind, and they can render suggestions without a full page refresh? What is that magic?”

# Guillermo Rauch: Why Software Development Will Never Be the Same
[The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck](https://podcasts.apple.com/gb/podcast/the-mad-podcast-with-matt-turck/id1686238724)
In this episode, Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch goes deep on how V0, their text-to-app platform, has already generated over 100 million applications and doubled Vercel’s user base in under a year.
Guillermo reveals how a tiny SWAT team inside Vercel built V0 from scratch, why “vibe coding” is making software creation accessible to everyone (not just engineers), and how the AI Cloud is automating DevOps, making cloud infrastructure self-healing, and letting companies expose their data to AI agents in just five lines of code.
You’ll hear why “every company will have to rethink itself as a token factory,” how Vercel’s Next.js went from a conference joke to powering Walmart, Nike, and Midjourney, and why the next billion app creators might not write a single line of code. Guillermo breaks down the difference between vibe coding and agentic engineering, shares wild stories of users building apps from napkin sketches, and explains how Vercel is infusing “taste” and best practices directly into their AI models.
We also dig into the business side: how Vercel’s AI-powered products are driving explosive growth, why retention and margins are strong, and how the company is adapting to a new wave of non-technical users. Plus: the future of MCP servers, the security challenges of agent-to-agent communication, and why prompting and AI literacy are now must-have skills.

February 4, 2026 • 43 mins

Guillermo shows how Vercel built skills.sh—a viral marketplace with over 34,000 community-submitted skills—using v0, and how the tool enables non-technical team members to contribute production-ready code changes. He walks through creating branches, implementing features, previewing changes, and submitting pull requests, all within v0.

Good morning everybody. Thank you GM and welcome back.
Hello. Today we're having the sixth annual Nex.js JS Conf. I'm pumped to spend a day with everyone here in this room. And a special shout out to the thousands of people watching around the world, especially our watch parties in London and Berlin. Thank you for spending your time with us today. Last year when we met, we're a year and a half into this app router era of Nex.js.
And at that time, our framework was being downloaded 7 million times per week. And over the course of building the app router, we made a lot of bets.
We'll be talking a lot about bets today.
When it first launched, it had the earliest mainstream implementation of React server components.
It also supported nested layouts. It used the file system as the API for its routing features. and it kicked off the development of TurboVac to ensure that this new server first architecture would scale to apps of all sizes.
Today, NexJS is being downloaded 13 million times per week, nearly double.
Nearly double the adoption in just one year, especially with AI math, right?
That tells us that many of our bets are paying off and we're so grateful to the community who has helped us get the app router to where it is today. Thank you so much.
But how we're writing software has changed. When we started the app router, GPT3 had just come out. ChatGpt or Chat didn't exist. GitHub Copilot was in beta. In a very short amount of time, we've gone from writing code by hand to getting suggestions to having models write code to now having agents that author, test, execute, and ship entire features.
And what we've seen with this LLMs is that they really push us in terms of the design of our own APIs.
An LLM's context window is even shorter than a human's attention span. So if we make an API that's confusing for anyone in this room, the LLMs stand no chance.
The easier we make the developer experience for humans, the better we make it for agents.
So with all of this in mind, let's take a closer look at our bets and see which ones paid off and which ones we're revisiting. So the first one we're really happy with, I'm really happy with is React Server Components.
When we decided to go allin on RSC's, we're investing in a novel unproven architecture.
Fast forward to today and we're seeing RSC's gain adoption in popular ecosystem projects like VIT, React Router, Redwood SDK, Wacku, Parcel, and more. and other projects like tan tans start are actively working on an integration.
